explanation: Article 39A of the Constitution directs the State to secure a legal system that promotes justice on the basis of equal opportunity and specifically provides free legal aid to the poor and needy. Article 43A directs the State to take steps to secure the participation of workers in the management of undertakings, establishments, or other organizations engaged in any industry.

Foreigners in India are granted Fundamental Rights like Equality before the Law (Article 14) and Protection of Life and Personal Liberty (Article 21). However, they are not entitled to Cultural and Educational Rights (Articles 29 and 30) or protection from discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth (Article 15), as these are generally for citizens.

The Right to Freedom of Speech and Expression is guaranteed under Article 19(1)(a). Immunity from Double Persecution is covered under Article 20. The Abolition of Titles is dealt with in Article 18. The Right to Legal Aid, while not a standalone fundamental right, is implied under Article 21 (Right to Life and Personal Liberty) through judicial interpretation.

Article 3 of the Indian Constitution empowers Parliament to form a new State by separating territory from any State, increasing the area of any State, diminishing the area of any State, or altering the name of any State. It does not grant Parliament the power to abolish a State by itself; states are formed and their boundaries can be altered.

The Indian Constitution clearly establishes a federal structure with a distribution of powers between the Union and the States, reflected in the three legislative lists: Union, State, and Concurrent. This distribution extends to legislative, financial, and administrative spheres, ensuring a balance of authority.

The Parliament has exclusive power to legislate on subjects enumerated in the Union List. For the Concurrent List, both Parliament and State Legislatures can make laws, but in case of a conflict, the law made by Parliament prevails over the law made by the State Legislature.

The executive powers of the Lok Sabha are exercised through mechanisms like Calling Attention Motions, Questions and Supplementary Questions, and the Zero Hour, which allow members to bring matters of public importance to the government's attention and seek information. A No Confidence Motion, while a powerful tool, is primarily a political mechanism to test the confidence of the House in the government, not a direct exercise of executive power by the Lok Sabha itself.

The 61st Amendment Act reduced the voting age from 21 to 18 years. The 86th Amendment Act made elementary education a fundamental right. The 97th Amendment Act provided constitutional status to co-operative societies. The 101st Amendment Act introduced the Goods and Services Tax (GST).

Article 368 of the Constitution explicitly empowers the Parliament to amend the Constitution. While Parliament can amend most parts, the Supreme Court, in the Kesavananda Bharati case, established the doctrine of the basic structure, meaning Parliament cannot amend the Constitution in a way that destroys its fundamental or basic features.

The 106th Constitution Amendment Act, 2023, provides for reservation of seats for women in the Lok Sabha and State Legislative Assemblies, and also for Scheduled Castes and Scheduled Tribes in these reserved seats. It does not restore control to state governments for identifying backward classes, nor does it relate to the representation of Scheduled Castes in the legislative council.

The 103rd Amendment Act of the Constitution of India introduced reservations for Economically Weaker Sections (EWS) in higher education and government jobs, amending Articles 15 and 16 of the Constitution.

The anti-defection law, primarily governed by the Tenth Schedule of the Constitution, applies to members of a political party, independent members (who would lose their seat if they joined a party after the election), and nominated members (who have a six-month grace period to join a party).

The Supreme Court of India directed the Election Commission to provide a 'None of the above' (NOTA) option in Electronic Voting Machines in 2013. The Parliament has not passed a specific law for the introduction of NOTA; it was a judicial pronouncement. If NOTA gets the highest votes, the candidate who secured the second-highest votes is declared elected, as NOTA does not lead to a re-election.

The Election Commission of India is a multi-member body consisting of a Chief Election Commissioner and two Election Commissioners, as established by the Representation of the People (Amendment) Act, 1993.

Part XV of the Constitution of India deals with Elections and establishes the Election Commission. The Election Commission was made a multi-member body by an Act of Parliament in 1993, not 1996. The Chief Election Commissioner and other Election Commissioners have a fixed tenure and are appointed by the President.
